title: Add Microsoft and GitHub Authentication
description: Implement multiple OAuth providers (Microsoft and GitHub) with a unified provider selection UI and account linking system.
instructions:
Add both Microsoft Account and GitHub OAuth providers to an existing ASP.NET Core Identity application. Implement a provider-agnostic challenge system that works with both providers and includes a selection UI.

**Requirements:**

1. **Configure Microsoft Account Authentication**
   - Add MicrosoftAccount authentication
   - ClientId and ClientSecret from Configuration
   - SaveTokens = true
   - Scopes: openid, email, profile, User.Read
   - Tenant = "common" (allows personal AND work/school accounts)
   - Log email in OnCreatingTicket event

2. **Configure GitHub Authentication**
   - Use generic OAuth handler (AddOAuth with scheme name "GitHub")
   - ClientId and ClientSecret from Configuration
   - Endpoints:
     - Authorization: https://github.com/login/oauth/authorize
     - Token: https://github.com/login/oauth/access_token
     - UserInfo: https://api.github.com/user
   - CallbackPath = "/signin-github"
   - Scopes: read:user, user:email
   - Map claims: id -> NameIdentifier, login -> Name, avatar_url -> ProfileImage
   - IMPORTANT: GitHub's user endpoint doesn't always return email
     - If email is null, fetch from https://api.github.com/user/emails
     - Find the primary email and add it to claims
   - Add User-Agent header (required by GitHub API)

3. **Create GET /api/auth/providers**
   - Return JSON array of available providers with:
     - name, displayName, icon, color for each provider
   - Use this to populate provider selection UI

4. **Create GET /api/auth/challenge/{provider}**
   - Accepts provider name (microsoft or github) in route
   - Query parameter: returnUrl
   - Validate provider name, return 400 if invalid
   - Challenge with appropriate scheme for the provider
   - Store returnUrl in AuthenticationProperties
   - Redirect to callback URL after OAuth flow

5. **Create GET /api/auth/callback/{provider}**
   - Handle callback from both providers uniformly
   - Get external login info
   - Extract email (handle GitHub's different claim structure)
   - Find or create user (same logic as Google challenge)
   - Link provider if not already linked
   - Redirect to returnUrl from query or /dashboard

6. **Create POST /api/account/link/{provider}**
   - Protected endpoint for adding providers to existing account
   - Challenge with specified provider
   - Redirect back to /account/connections or specified URL

7. **Create GET /api/account/suggested-providers**
   - Protected endpoint returning providers NOT linked to current user
   - Also return linked providers list and hasPassword flag

testCases.description: Should validate provider names in challenge endpoint
Should add User-Agent header for GitHub API

hints.text: Use builder.Services.AddAuthentication().AddMicrosoftAccount() for Microsoft - it has built-in configuration.
For GitHub, use .AddOAuth('GitHub', 'GitHub', options => { ... }) as there's no built-in handler.
GitHub requires User-Agent header on all API requests. Add it in OnCreatingTicket when calling user info.
Fetch additional emails from https://api.github.com/user/emails if the user endpoint returns null email.
Use pattern matching or a dictionary to map provider names to their authentication scheme names.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not adding User-Agent header for GitHub API calls
commonMistakes.consequence: GitHub returns 403 Forbidden with 'Request forbidden by administrative rules' error.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not handling GitHub's separate emails endpoint
commonMistakes.consequence: Email claim is null for users with private emails, breaking user lookup/creation.
commonMistakes.correction: If user endpoint email is null, call /user/emails and find the primary email.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using wrong tenant for Microsoft Account
commonMistakes.consequence: Only work accounts allowed, or only personal accounts, instead of both.
commonMistakes.correction: Set options.Tenant = 'common' to allow both Microsoft personal and work/school accounts.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not validating provider names before challenging
commonMistakes.consequence: Users can pass arbitrary provider names causing exceptions or unexpected behavior.
commonMistakes.correction: Validate provider against allowed list and return 400 for invalid providers.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not handling different claim mappings per provider
commonMistakes.consequence: Name or email claims return null because you're looking for wrong claim types.
commonMistakes.correction: GitHub uses 'login' for username, Microsoft uses standard claim types. Map appropriately.
